name: VTC Martin Display
classification_notes: Derived directly from Hamilton Gothic No. 6243, a wood type grotesque from Hamilton's 1906 catalogue, with additional weights drawn from related Hamilton Gothic typefaces; the letterpress origin and protest placard source material place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A wood type grotesque revival drawn directly from the letterpress 'I AM A MAN' placards carried during the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike, digitised as a variable font with italics.
description: The letterforms originate from Hamilton Gothic No. 6243, a typeface from Hamilton's 1906 wood type catalogue, identified through a chance discovery of a 1960 Hamilton Wood Type specimen. Additional weights were drawn from related Hamilton Gothic faces, with punctuation sourced from early 1960s headlines of The Baltimore Afro-American Newspaper. VTC Martin Display stays closest to the original placard forms, bold in its uppercase and lighter in its lowercase, and now includes variable italics.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe VTC Martin Display as the family that stays closest to the original protest placards, preserving the bold uppercase and lighter lowercase of the letterpress source. First released in June 2016 as the very first Vocal Type font, it has since grown into a variable font with italics, part of a two-family release alongside the more versatile VTC Martin. The foundry frame each character as carrying the DNA of resistance, transforming ephemeral protest into a living communication tool.

name: VTC Martin
classification_notes: Letterforms derived directly from letterpress Hamilton Gothic No. 6243 wood type, as used on the 'I AM A MAN' protest placards of 1968; a display gothic with historical wood type lineage.
summary: A display gothic family drawn from the letterpress 'I AM A MAN' placards of the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike, tracing its letterforms back to Hamilton Gothic No. 6243 from 1906.
description: The letterforms originate from a 1960 Hamilton Wood Type catalogue discovered on eBay, which identified the typeface used on the protest signs as Gothic No. 6243. Additional weights were drawn from other Hamilton Gothic typefaces, and punctuation was sourced from early 1960s issues of The Baltimore Afro-American Newspaper. First released in June 2016 as Vocal Type's debut font, the family has since grown into two subfamilies: VTC Martin, with variable weights and italics, and VTC Martin Display, which preserves the original's bold uppercase and lighter lowercase contrast.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe VTC Martin as a font family born from the crucible of protest, with letterforms derived from the iconic 'I AM A MAN' placards carried during the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s final campaign for economic justice. They present it as a living communication tool that transforms ephemeral resistance into type, preserving protest in letterform so that each character continues a conversation about human worth begun by those sanitation workers.

name: VTC Wij
classification_notes: Drawn from handmade protest banners, the letterforms are upright with near-uniform stroke weight and consistent width across characters, placing it firmly in the display category with a hand-lettered subcategory rooted in activist typography.
summary: A display typeface drawn from a 1988 Dutch protest banner made during the campaign to rehabilitate anti-colonial writer and resistance fighter Anton de Kom.
description: The letterforms are derived from archival sources, primarily a banner produced by LOSON during their eerherstel campaign for Anton de Kom, and cross-referenced against Dutch protest materials spanning the 1960s to 1980s. Strokes are upright and near-uniform in weight, with each character occupying roughly the same horizontal space regardless of internal structure, reflecting the logic of hand-lettering made to be read at scale across large crowds. The Dutch digraph IJ is included as a single glyph, drawn from banners produced by the feminist coalition Wij Vrouwen Eisen.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe VTC Wij as drawn from a 1988 protest banner made during the campaign for the formal rehabilitation of Anton de Kom, a Surinamese-Dutch anti-colonial writer and resistance fighter who died in German detention weeks before liberation. The typeface was first deployed in a 105-metre mural in Breda, painted by more than sixty volunteers including de Kom's grandson, featuring his declaration Vandaag vrij, altijd vrij. The letterforms were reconstructed by identifying structural consistency across multiple handmade sources, including four variations of the uppercase S, of which three were retained, and the IJ digraph drawn from Wij Vrouwen Eisen banners.

name: VTC Garibaldi
classification_notes: An extra condensed grotesque sans with a mechanical, wooden-type character drawn from Italian anti-fascist WWII print ephemera; structured and utilitarian in nature.
summary: An extra condensed grotesque sans rooted in the clandestine anti-fascist print culture of wartime Italy, designed by Vocal Type in collaboration with Michele Patanè.
description: Drawn from the mastheads, flyers and journals of the Italian Resistance, particularly the Brigate Garibaldi's publication Il Combattente (1943–1944), VTC Garibaldi Sans Extra Condensed reproduces the mechanical, sturdy character of wooden headline type used under conditions of secrecy and scarcity. The family is conceived as a broader typographic palette eventually comprising a sans, a serif and a monospace, each reflecting the eclectic range of type available to clandestine printers during the German occupation. The current release focuses on the condensed sans cut, with an extended variant in development.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe VTC Garibaldi as a family inspired by the typographic production of anti-fascist material distributed during WWII, from flyers and posters to partisan journals, all made in clandestinity using whatever printing technology was at hand. The project is a collaboration with Michele Patanè and draws specifically on the masthead of Il Combattente, the journal of the Brigate Garibaldi, as a key reference for the extra condensed grotesque currently available. The planned family will eventually include a serif and monospace to complete a toolkit reflecting the full typographic palette of the Italian Resistance.

name: VTC Tank Man
classification_notes: The typeface is drawn from protest banner lettering photographed at the 1989 Tiananmen Square demonstrations, placing it firmly in the display category as a historically derived, expressive hand-lettered sans.
summary: A display typeface drawn from protest banners carried during the 1989 Tiananmen Square demonstrations, part of Vocal Type's ongoing series connecting type history to political movements.
description: The letterforms are sourced from two banners photographed from multiple angles during the peaceful 1989 Tiananmen Square protests in Beijing, one carrying the phrase 'Give me democracy or give me death.' The trial version incorporates a torch adapted from features of both the banner lettering and the student-built Goddess of Democracy sculpture that was destroyed by military vehicles on 4 June 1989. Like other Vocal Type releases, it situates typeface design within a specific historical and political context, making the act of using the font an engagement with that history.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type present VTC Tank Man as a typeface drawn directly from protest banner lettering carried during the 1989 Tiananmen Square demonstrations in Beijing. The foundry situates the design within the broader history of the protests, noting the students' deliberate borrowing of slogans from the Civil Rights Movement and the Black Panther Party, and the creation and destruction of the Goddess of Democracy sculpture. The trial version's torch is adapted from both the banner and the sculpture.

name: VTC Tatsuro
classification_notes: Inspired by hand-lettered signage from a 1941 photograph, VTC Tatsuro draws from vernacular display lettering of the early 1940s, placing it firmly in the display category with a historical, hand-crafted character.
summary: A display typeface inspired by the hand-lettered 'I AM AN AMERICAN' sign commissioned by Tatsuro Matsuda on his Oakland grocery store the day after Pearl Harbor, before his family were forcibly interned under Executive Order 9066.
description: Vocal Type draws directly from the vernacular lettering of a 1941 storefront sign, immortalised in Dorothea Lange's iconic photograph of Japanese American internment. The typeface carries the historical weight of that moment, connecting display typography to one of the most documented injustices of wartime America. Vocal Type's practice of grounding typefaces in marginalised histories gives VTC Tatsuro a specificity and purpose that purely formal display faces rarely achieve.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type recount the story of Tatsuro Matsuda, a University of California graduate who commissioned and installed a large sign reading 'I AM AN AMERICAN' on his family's Wanto Co grocery store in Oakland on 8 December 1941, the day after the attack on Pearl Harbor. The store was soon closed as the Matsuda family were relocated and incarcerated under the US government's Japanese American internment policy. The typeface is inspired by that sign, which was photographed by documentary photographer Dorothea Lange and became one of the most iconic images of Japanese internment during WWII.

name: Bayard
classification_notes: Categorised as sans-serif with a display orientation, drawn from hand-lettered protest signage from the 1963 March on Washington rather than any conventional type tradition.
summary: A sans-serif drawn from protest signs at the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, named after organiser Bayard Rustin.
description: Vocal Type's Bayard takes its letterforms directly from signage carried during the 1963 March on Washington, one of the defining moments of the American civil rights movement. The typeface is named after Bayard Rustin, the march's deputy director and a principal architect of nonviolent protest strategy, who also guided the event's organisation in under two months despite internal opposition. It sits in a body of work from Vocal Type that grounds typeface design in specific Black historical narratives rather than generic Americana.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe Bayard as a unique sans-serif inspired by signs from the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, and name it after Bayard Rustin, a close advisor to Martin Luther King and one of the most effective organisers of the civil rights movement across the 1940s through 1960s, who also advocated publicly for gay and lesbian causes in the 1980s.

name: VTC Harriet
classification_notes: VTC Harriet is a symbol and pattern typeface in which each character resolves to a quilt block motif, functioning as a modular design system rather than a readable text face. It sits firmly in the display/ornamental category.
summary: A symbol typeface in which every character maps to a quilt block pattern drawn from the Underground Railroad Quilt Code, named after Harriet Tubman.
description: Each letter and numeral resolves to a single quilt block, regular or inverted, allowing designers to compose repeating patterns by typing. Stylistic sets extend the system into pre-built 2x2 through 5x5 arrangements, and alternating ligature pairs produce checkerboard rhythms automatically. The typeface is grounded in the oral tradition of the Underground Railroad Quilt Code, in which specific quilt patterns communicated directions and instructions to people escaping enslavement.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type frame VTC Harriet around the Underground Railroad Quilt Code, an oral tradition passed down since Antebellum times and first publicly recited by Ozella McDaniel Williams of Charleston, South Carolina. Each glyph is a quilt block that can be combined to produce any pattern the designer chooses, with stylistic sets offering ready-made 2x2 to 5x5 arrangements. The typeface is named after Harriet Tubman, the escaped enslaved woman who became a conductor on the Underground Railroad and later served as a nurse, Union spy and women's suffrage supporter.

name: Du Bois
classification_notes: An uppercase-first geometric sans with no curves, drawn directly from W.E.B. Du Bois's hand-lettered infographic charts. The rigid, rectilinear letterforms with multiple widths and slanted rectangles in place of italics place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A geometric sans drawn from the hand-lettered infographic charts produced by W.E.B. Du Bois for the 1900 Paris World's Fair, expanded by Vocal Type into a full family with lowercase, multiple widths and stylistic alternates.
description: The letterforms originate in Du Bois's near-60 poster-sized statistical charts, which used an uppercase sans with no curves across two weights and three widths, with occasional slanted-rectangle italics and reverse italics. Vocal Type has extended that source material to include lowercase characters, eleven sets of stylistic alternates, and map key ornaments drawn from the infographics themselves. The result sits at the intersection of historical revival and contemporary type design, carrying the visual language of a body of work that predated Bauhaus geometric ambitions by nearly two decades.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type frames Du Bois as a revival of the hand-drawn letterforms used in W.E.B. Du Bois's landmark sociological infographics, created to bring those achievements to the attention of the wider design industry. The original charts were uppercase-only with no curves, two weights and three widths; the typeface expands on that foundation with lowercase, stylistic alternates and refined map key characters.

name: Marsha
classification_notes: Inspired by the vertical sign outside the Stonewall Inn, Marsha is a display typeface rooted in mid-century American signage lettering, making it a historically grounded display face rather than a text or body type.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the vertical sign of the Stonewall Inn and named in honour of transgender activist Marsha P. Johnson.
description: Rooted in the lettering of the Stonewall Inn's original vertical sign, this typeface carries the visual memory of a site central to LGBT liberation in America. Vocal Type named it after Marsha P. Johnson, an African-American transgender activist whose role in the 1969 Stonewall uprising brought her to the forefront of the gay liberation movement. The design is part of Vocal Type's broader practice of recovering type histories tied to civil rights and social movements.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describes Marsha as a typeface inspired by the vertical sign that once hung outside the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village, named after Marsha P. Johnson, an African-American transgender woman from New Jersey whose activism during the 1960s and 70s made her one of the most prominent figures in the Stonewall uprising of 1969.

name: Carrie
classification_notes: The typeface is presented as a display face rooted in historical suffragist visual culture, likely drawing on period lettering from early twentieth-century American protest and political campaign materials.
summary: A display typeface inspired by the lettering of American women's suffrage campaigns, named after strategist and activist Carrie Chapman Catt.
description: Named after Carrie Chapman Catt, the suffragist who led the 1915 Fifth Avenue parade and steered the Nineteenth Amendment to ratification in 1920, this typeface draws on the visual language of early twentieth-century American political activism. Vocal Type's practice of grounding type designs in specific historical moments of social struggle gives Carrie a cultural weight beyond its letterforms. It suits editorial and campaign work where that historical reference is intentional.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type situate Carrie within the history of American women's suffrage, tracing the movement from the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention through to the 1915 New York parade led by Carrie Chapman Catt, who later secured passage of the Nineteenth Amendment in 1920. The typeface is named in her honour.

name: EVA
classification_notes: Inspired by hand-lettered protest banners from a 1957 Buenos Aires demonstration, the typeface carries the informal, expressive character typical of display type drawn from activist and vernacular lettering sources.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the hand-lettered banners of a 1957 women's suffrage demonstration in Buenos Aires, part of Vocal Type's broader project recovering type from marginalised historical movements.
description: Rooted in the lettering of Argentine suffragist protest, the typeface takes its name and spirit from Eva Perón, whose campaigning was central to the passage of Argentina's women's suffrage law. Vocal Type draws from primary visual sources of activist movements, treating each typeface as a piece of social history rather than a purely aesthetic exercise. The result carries the weight and directness of hand-made signage while functioning as a usable display family.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe EVA as inspired by banners carried during a 1957 women's demonstration in Buenos Aires, held in front of the National Congress in support of universal suffrage. The family is named in honour of Eva Perón, who was largely responsible for the passage of Argentina's women's suffrage law and founded the Peronista Feminist Party in 1949.

name: Ruben
classification_notes: Described as inspired by remnants of 1970s Chicano protest materials, suggesting a historically-grounded display typeface with editorial and activist visual heritage.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the visual remnants of the 1970 National Chicano Moratorium, honouring journalist Rubén Salazar.
description: Vocal Type's Ruben takes its formal cues from lettering found in materials connected to the 1970 National Chicano Moratorium march in East Los Angeles, where 25,000 activists gathered to protest the Vietnam War. The typeface is named for Rubén Salazar, a journalist and advocate for Latino rights who was killed by Los Angeles Sheriff's deputies during the march. It sits within Vocal Type's broader project of recovering and archiving the typographic heritage of civil rights movements.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe Ruben as a font inspired by journalist Rubén Salazar and the visual remnants of the 1970 National Chicano Moratorium, a protest march in East Los Angeles where Salazar was killed when sheriff's deputies fired a tear gas projectile that struck him in the head. No one was ever arrested in connection with his death.

name: The Neue Black
classification_notes: A display typeface drawn from the hand-lettered signage of the Chicago Freedom Movement; its bold, blocky character and historical grounding place it firmly in the display category rather than a conventional text sans.
summary: A free display typeface by Vocal Type drawn from the signage of Martin Luther King Jr's Chicago Freedom Movement, released for Black History Month 2020 in collaboration with The Art of Blackness.
description: Created for Black History Month 2020 in partnership with The Art of Blackness, the typeface takes its letterforms from the signage of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference's 1966 Chicago Freedom Movement, the campaign that pushed civil rights activism into northern American cities. A single Black weight reflects the monumental, declarative quality of protest graphics. Available as a free download, with the option to support Vocal Type financially.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type collaborated with The Art of Blackness, an annual exhibition connecting African American artists with design professionals, to produce this typeface based on signage from the Chicago Freedom Movement. The SCLC's 1966 northern campaign, led by Martin Luther King Jr, forms the historical backbone of the design, which was released free to download for Black History Month 2020.

name: VTC Spike
classification_notes: A display typeface characterised by square geometric counters drawn from film poster lettering and jersey numerals, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric sensibility.
summary: A three-style display family designed by Tré Seals to accompany his full book design for Spike Lee's monograph, drawing on jersey numerals, film posters and the iconic rings from Do The Right Thing.
description: Designed in parallel with a five-typeface custom commission for Spike Lee's book, VTC Spike distils the visual language of Lee's career into a single headline family. Square geometric counters run through the entire design, sourced from a typographic tone board of film poster lettering; the numerals reference jersey graphics from the Knicks, Michael Jordan and Jackie Robinson. The result is a typeface that reads as both a portrait of its subject and a considered piece of Afro-American cultural type design.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describes VTC Spike as a family with three styles released alongside the Spike Lee monograph on 17 November 2021, based on the primary headline typeface Tré Seals designed for the book. Inspiration draws on the LOVE and HATE rings from Do The Right Thing, the MARS chain from She's Gotta Have It, Michael Jordan Nike commercials, and the geometric counters found consistently across Spike Lee's film posters and logos.

name: Colorful
designer: Tré Seals, Rich Tu, Sunday Afternoon
classification_notes: The typeface draws its letterforms from the shape of the visible light spectrum, giving it a conceptually driven geometric quality suited to display use rather than text setting.
summary: A display typeface shaped by the visible light spectrum, created for the COLORFUL grant programme supporting young BIPOC creatives.
description: Designed in collaboration with Sunday Afternoon for the third year of the COLORFUL identity, the letterforms take their cues from the diagram of the visible light spectrum as a formal metaphor for a breadth of perspectives. Proceeds from sales go directly to the COLORFUL grant programme run by Rich Tu and The One Club for Creativity. The conceptual rigour behind the forms gives it a distinctive voice beyond a typical identity commission.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type collaborated with the team at Sunday Afternoon to create this typeface for the COLORFUL identity, a global portfolio competition and grant programme supporting young BIPOC creatives. The design is rooted in the visible light spectrum, with letterforms drawn from its shape to reflect the programme's ethos of highlighting a spectrum of perspectives. Proceeds from font sales are donated to help grow the COLORFUL grant programme.

name: JAMES
classification_notes: JAMES is explicitly described as a stencil font family, placing it firmly in the display category with a stencil subcategory, designed for expressive headline and signage use.
summary: A stencil family drawn from protest signs carried during demonstrations that contributed to Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, designed by Vocal Type as part of their ongoing project to recover Black typographic history.
description: JAMES takes its reference from signage used during CORE's 1963 campaign against Jefferson Bank and Trust in St. Louis, a seven-month protest against discriminatory hiring practices that became the largest civil rights struggle in the city's history. Vocal Type's practice of grounding typefaces in specific moments of Black American history gives JAMES a documentary weight beyond its stencil letterforms. The result sits naturally in editorial, campaign and commemorative contexts where historical specificity matters.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type describe JAMES as a stencil font family inspired by signs carried during demonstrations that helped bring about Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, connecting the typeface directly to CORE's protests against unequal hiring practices at Jefferson Bank and Trust in St. Louis.

name: VTC Ruby
classification_notes: VTC Ruby is a decorative inline display type, descended from 19th-century ornamented gothics historically sold under names like Gothic Shade and English Ornamented. Its heavy strokes and shadow-inline structure place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A revival of the 19th-century inline display typeface historically known as Gothic Shade, renamed in honour of Ruby Bridges to sever it from its toxic ATF-era designation.
description: The typeface traces a complicated lineage from a French foundry to the Dickinson Type Foundry in 1850s Boston, before passing into the American Type Founders catalogue where it was recast in 1933 and 1949 under the deeply offensive name Jim Crow. Vocal Type's revival introduces design refinements alongside the new name, chosen to honour Ruby Bridges, who in 1960 became the first Black child to integrate a Southern elementary school. The result is an ornamented inline display face whose history is now anchored to a story of resistance rather than oppression.
foundry_blurb: Vocal Type recounts the typeface's troubled journey from a stolen French design through the Dickinson and ATF foundries, where it was recast under a name tied to racial caricature and segregation-era law. The revival, renamed VTC Ruby after Ruby Bridges, corrects that history and redirects the typeface's legacy toward the civil rights movement she came to represent at just six years old.
